metric_value: [The enterprise software market is] way too complex, with way too many suppliers in the corporate kitchen, so to speak.
notes: Kastner as director of research Aberdeen Group; NYT quote

metric_value: But you can be sure that Microsoft is heading inexorably upstream. And keeping Microsoft from gaining a greater share of the data center market is a crucial strategic objective for Oracle.
notes: Kastner NYT quote; core Oracle-strategy framing

metric_value: Oracle announced a $5.1 billion hostile takeover bid for PeopleSoft the prior week; rare hostile takeover in software industry; Ellison expressed little interest in PeopleSoft products, more in customers.
notes: June 6 2003 bid announcement referenced in NYT

metric_value: Microsoft was busily competing with Lotus in spreadsheets and it was unsuccessful. It was competing against WordPerfect in word processing, and Microsoft was unsuccessful. Eventually, Office came out and Microsoft dominated.
notes: Ellison NYT interview quote; applications-bundle playbook

metric_value: SAP, a big German company, is the market leader in business software. Its stronghold is in software for accounting and procurement. But it is also gaining strongly as a vendor of human resources programs, where PeopleSoft has done well, and in customer relationship management software, where Siebel Systems is the leader.
After 18-month hostile bid battle, Oracle closed $10.3B acquisition of PeopleSoft in January 2005; PeopleSoft applications continued under Oracle Applications Unlimited.
Oracle acquired Siebel Systems for $5.85B in January 2006, completing CRM consolidation Kastner predicted.
Microsoft pushed successfully into data center via SQL Server, Dynamics ERP/CRM, Azure SQL, Power BI, and Fabric; became a top-three enterprise database vendor — validating Kastner prediction.
